# Test fixture: canonical threat-model excerpt

Mirrors the post-AF-2026-05-10-2 T-MTG-5 row plus surrounding
T-MTG-1..T-MTG-4 rows for format-precedent context.

| STRIDE | Threat ID | Threat | Applicable? | Details |
|--------|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|
| **I** — Information Disclosure | T-MTG-1 | Web-search query exfiltration of charter context | Yes | The WebSearch / WebFetch query string under `/gaia-meeting` research phase carries charter sentence content (which often includes proprietary roadmap, customer name, or strategic intent) to the search provider. Search-provider logs are out-of-band of the framework's audit trail. **Likelihood:** medium-high (web-search is the default for any topic that mentions an external system). **Impact:** medium (charter content typically encodes business intent rather than secrets, but a sensitive-context meeting can leak the existence of an unannounced product line, an active acquisition target, or a pre-disclosure security-incident response). **Mitigation:** (a) `--no-web` flag disables web search entirely for sensitive contexts; (b) FR-MTG-6 records the meeting frontmatter `web_search: enabled | disabled` for retrospective review; (c) the SKILL.md research-phase guidance documents the search-query exfiltration model and recommends `--no-web` for charters that touch unannounced products, customers, or active incidents; (d) a future opt-in allowlist of approved search domains (e.g., only `*.anthropic.com`, `*.k8s.io`, `*.rfc-editor.org`) is reserved for a follow-up ADR; (e) NFR-MTG-2 cite-or-flag verifiability ensures every web-sourced claim carries a URL — the audit trail at least lists which domains were queried, even if the queries themselves are unrecorded. **Residual risk:** Medium. **Test:** TC-MTG-RESEARCH-5 (--no-web disables web invocations), TC-MTG-RESEARCH-6 (cite-or-flag verifiability over saved meeting file). |
| **T** — Tampering / **E** — Elevation of Privilege (via influence) | T-MTG-2 | Prompt-injection from attacker-controlled external pages | Yes | Pages returned by WebFetch contain attacker-controlled text. An attacker who controls a domain that an agent's research-phase web search returns (or an attacker who hijacks a legitimate site) can inject prompt-injection content into the page body. The agent reads the content and incorporates it into its prelude and DISCUSS turns. The injection can attempt to (a) flip the agent's recommendation, (b) instruct the agent to omit a critical concern, (c) instruct the agent to mark its own claims as `[inference]` to bypass cite-or-flag scrutiny. **Likelihood:** medium (prompt-injection-via-search is a known pattern in agent-driven workflows). **Impact:** medium (affects judgment, not write boundary — fork allowlist remains read-only; meeting writes only to creative-artifacts + action-items + sidecar-decisions). **Mitigation:** (a) cite-or-flag invariant (FR-MTG-5 / NFR-MTG-2) — every factual claim must cite a source; an injected instruction that doesn't cite a source is detectable in the transcript; (b) the structured prelude format (`Sources consulted:` block) makes the source list reviewable before DISCUSS starts; (c) a user-interjection at the prelude review point catches obviously-influenced preludes (the user labels are visible per FR-MTG-10); (d) `--no-web` disables the web-injection surface entirely; (e) per-agent token cap (FR-MTG-29) bounds the runaway-influence damage — an injected agent muted on cap doesn't continue to amplify the injection through subsequent turns; (f) the sequential-turn invariant (FR-MTG-8) means injection on one agent's turn does not silently propagate to other agents' next turns without going through the user-visible shared log. **Residual risk:** Medium. **Test:** TC-MTG-RESEARCH-3 (uncited claim flagged), TC-MTG-GUARD-1 (cite-or-flag violation HALT), TC-MTG-RESEARCH-1 (prelude format with sources block). |
| **I** — Information Disclosure | T-MTG-3 | Charter-driven over-broad agent file reads exposing sensitive content | Yes | The research phase grants every agent the canonical fork allowlist `[Read, Grep, Glob, Bash]`. The fork is read-only (NFR-048), but the *scope* of the read is a function of the charter the user wrote. A charter such as "Review our secret-handling posture across the codebase" can lead an agent to enumerate `**/secrets/**`, `**/.env*`, or files matching common-credential patterns — and to land verbatim secret values into the prelude turn (which then lands in the meeting transcript at `docs/creative-artifacts/meeting-{date}-{slug}.md`). **Likelihood:** medium (charters that mention "credentials", "secrets", "auth", or "config" naturally steer agents toward sensitive paths). **Impact:** high (saved meeting files are committed to the project tree alongside other creative artifacts; secret material in a meeting transcript is now persisted in version control). **Mitigation:** (a) the `/gaia-meeting` SKILL.md research-phase guidance documents the charter-scope responsibility and recommends explicit "exclude" directives in the charter for sensitive-domain meetings (e.g., "Do not enumerate file contents of any path matching `**/secrets/**` or `**/.env*`"); (b) a pre-save scrubber pass runs the meeting transcript through a `gitleaks`-equivalent pattern matcher before persisting to disk; matches result in a `[REDACTED]` placeholder + a CRITICAL finding surfaced to the user during the SAVE phase; (c) the fork tool allowlist remains `[Read, Grep, Glob, Bash]` (NFR-048 preserved) — agents cannot write back to the project tree from the research fork; (d) `_memory/{agent}-sidecar/decisions/` write-throughs are produced at CLOSE under user REVIEW (FR-MTG-24) — the user can drop any agent's entry that contains sensitive content before the write lands. **Residual risk:** Medium. **Test:** TC-MTG-RESEARCH-3 (cite-or-flag still gates uncited content), TC-MTG-MEM-3 (REVIEW-phase drop prevents memory write), plus a new TC-MTG-SCRUB-1 covering the pre-save secret-pattern scrubber. |
| **I** — Information Disclosure | T-MTG-4 | Session-state file persists in-flight charter and scratchpad context outside the audited artifact tree (AF-2026-05-08-1) | Yes | FR-MTG-33 (added under AF-2026-05-08-1) introduces a fourth authorized write surface: `_memory/meeting-sessions/{YYYY-MM-DD}-{slug}.yaml`. The session file persists an in-flight charter (before user has reviewed it at SAVE), the cumulative scratchpad-state digest (which may reference unredacted scratchpad content from `meeting-scratchpad/`), the raise-hand ledger, and `cumulative_cost`. Unlike the saved meeting transcript at `docs/creative-artifacts/meeting-{date}-{slug}.md`, the session file is not subject to the pre-save secret-pattern scrubber documented under T-MTG-3 (the scrubber runs at SAVE; session state is written at every checkpoint yield, before SAVE). **Likelihood:** low (`_memory/` is local-only and not committed to git per the existing memory layout convention; session files do not propagate to remote unless the user deliberately commits `_memory/`). **Impact:** medium (a developer who shares their `_memory/` tarball, a backup that captures the directory, or an `rsync` of the home directory can leak in-flight meeting context — including charter content for sensitive meetings — that the user expected to remain private until SAVE-time scrubbing). **Mitigation:** (a) `_memory/` follows the same gitignore convention as the rest of the framework's local-only memory tier — session files inherit that boundary; (b) the framework's existing checkpoint reaper sweeps `_memory/checkpoints/` after a retention window per E43 conventions — FR-MTG-33 specifies a 30-day reap of session files (`_memory/meeting-sessions/`) after CLOSE/SAVE, which limits the leak window; (c) the SKILL.md MUST document, at the post-CHARTER yield, that the charter has been written to `_memory/meeting-sessions/` and that `--no-web` and explicit-exclude directives in the charter (per T-MTG-1, T-MTG-3 mitigations) should be applied before that yield for sensitive contexts; (d) the pre-save scrubber (T-MTG-3 mitigation b) MUST also run at the pre-CLOSE checkpoint yield to catch sensitive content before the session file persists across a checkpoint boundary; (e) FR-MTG-31 amended write-boundary explicitly enumerates `_memory/meeting-sessions/` so the boundary remains auditable. **Residual risk:** Low. **Test:** TC-MTG-CHKPT-5 (session-state schema), TC-MTG-CHKPT-7 (write-boundary extension), plus a follow-up test (deferred to E76-S7 hardening) covering the pre-CLOSE scrubber pass over the session file. |
| — | T-MTG-5 | User-as-first-class-attendee path under AF-2026-05-10-2 — verification (positive) | No (positive) | FR-MTG-10 amended AF-2026-05-10-2 introduces a user-as-first-class-attendee path: when `me` / `user` / a token resolving to the user's name appears in `--invitees`, the user is added as a non-LLM attendee with a turn slot at every yield boundary (composes with the AF-2026-05-10-1 / FR-MTG-32 amendment AskUserQuestion 5-option primitive). Verification: **no new threat surface.** (a) The user is non-LLM — no new prompt-injection surface (T-MTG-2 surface unchanged; user-as-attendee turns originate from the user, not from external pages). (b) User responses go through the substrate-enforced AskUserQuestion primitive already mitigated by E76-S18 / FR-MTG-32 amendment — the same substrate halt that closes the auto-mode-bypass class for /gaia-meeting yield boundaries. (c) Write boundary unchanged — the new `user_attendance: true | false` session-state flag lands in the existing `_memory/meeting-sessions/{YYYY-MM-DD}-{slug}.yaml` write surface (T-MTG-4 surface; same gitignore + 30-day reap mitigations apply). (d) The user-as-attendee path does not alter the canonical fork allowlist `[Read, Grep, Glob, Bash]` (NFR-048 preserved) — agents still cannot write back to the project tree from research forks. (e) The cite-or-flag invariant (NFR-MTG-2) continues to apply — the user's [i]nterject free-text response is recorded with origin marker in the persisted transcript. **Residual risk:** None additional beyond the pre-existing T-MTG-1..T-MTG-4 surfaces. **Test:** TC-MTG-USER-* family (added in E76-S21) covers the user-as-attendee composition with AskUserQuestion + session-state user_attendance flag; TC-MTG-NOFAB-2 (description-reconciled per E76-S21) and TC-MTG-NOFAB-3a (PRIMARY trace_owner E76-S21) verify the carve-out preserves the auto-emit-only invariant for unsolicited / fabricated user turns. |
